title: Russia Pummels Kyiv in Largest Barrage of the War, Iran Preps Six-Day Funeral as Doha Talks Tread Water, Oil Swings to Glut Risk
subhead: Russia unleashed its most intense attack on Kyiv in four years, killing at least 27. Iran begins six days of funeral ceremonies for Khamenei as US-Iran technical talks in Doha yield limited progress. Oil prices have fallen below $71/barrel as Hormuz traffic rebounds, with analysts warning of an oversupply glut. NATO leaders prepare to gather in Ankara amid the backdrop of an accelerating European rearmament push.

sections.heading: Russia's Largest Barrage Devastates Kyiv
sections.body:
Russia launched its most intense missile and drone attack on Kyiv overnight on July 2, deploying 74 missiles and 496 drones across several waves lasting more than 11 hours, according to the Ukrainian Air Force. At least 27 people were killed and 91 injured, Kyiv's city administration reported. Mayor Vitaly Klitschko called it the 'most massive' attack on the capital in terms of the volume of weapons used, even if prior attacks had higher single-event death tolls.

The barrage hit residential buildings, an ambulance station, a scientific institute, a hotel, and a publishing house where approximately 800,000 books were destroyed. A missile crater opened next to a kindergarten. Over 52,500 people sheltered in metro stations overnight — the highest number in recent years, according to city authorities. Finland imposed a temporary aviation restriction zone in its eastern Gulf, and Poland scrambled fighter jets in response to the attack's spillover risk.

The Ukrainian Air Force said it downed or neutralized 48 missiles and 476 drones, but 25 ballistic missiles and 12 drones still struck 33 locations. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov stated Russia would 'continue to increase pressure on the Kyiv regime.' President Zelenskyy, who cut short a trip to Ireland to return to Kyiv, said Ukraine would 'definitely' retaliate, noting that Russia's head is 'completely refusing to end the war.' The strike comes as Ukraine has stepped up its own long-range drone campaign against Russian oil refineries and military facilities, with ISW recording at least 303 intermediate-range strikes against Russian targets in occupied Ukraine in June alone.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of this attack — the largest weapons volume directed at Kyiv in the war — signals that Putin is escalating rather than seeking off-ramps, even as Ukraine's deep-strike campaign imposes growing costs on Russia's energy infrastructure.

sections.heading: Iran Begins Khamenei Funeral as Doha Talks Make Incremental Progress
sections.body:
Six-day funeral ceremonies for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed in the opening US-Israeli strikes on February 28, begin July 4 in Tehran and culminate in his burial in Mashhad on July 9. Iranian authorities are mobilizing for what acting Tehran Mayor Alireza Zakani described as 'the largest assembly in the history of the capital,' with businesses closed and work suspended across the city for three official days of mourning. A funeral procession through Iraq's holy cities of Najaf and Karbala on July 8 is widely seen as a signal of Iran's regional influence. DW reports that Iran's leadership is using the funeral to project national cohesion, though the war has deepened disillusionment, particularly among younger Iranians.

Meanwhile, US-Iran indirect technical talks in Doha concluded on Wednesday with limited results. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi, who headed Tehran's delegation, said two meetings addressed US violations of the Islamabad Memorandum and the release of $6 billion in frozen Iranian funds. A communication channel to resolve disputes was established. Qatar's foreign ministry spokesman Majed al-Ansari said 'positive progress' was made, and Pakistan issued an identical statement. Both sides agreed to schedule the next meeting after Khamenei's funeral. Iran's parliamentary speaker Ghalibaf denied that the IAEA had access to bombed nuclear sites at Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan, stating that inspectors are currently limited to the Bushehr power plant and the Tehran reactor, in defiance of IAEA chief Rafael Grossi's insistence on access.
sections.whyItMatters: The funeral is both a genuine national mourning event and a carefully choreographed display of regime resilience. The Doha talks produced procedural gains — a communication channel, progress on frozen funds — but no breakthrough on the core issues of nuclear inspections or a permanent peace deal. The pause for the funeral buys both sides time but also introduces a volatile period where large public gatherings could become flashpoints.

sections.heading: From Shortage to Glut: Oil Prices Drop Below $71 as Hormuz Traffic Rebounds
sections.body:
Brent crude fell to $70.78 a barrel on July 2, down 1.1 percent, while US WTI dropped to $67.74 — both extending losses for a third consecutive session, according to Al Jazeera and Trading Economics data. The price decline comes as commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has rebounded faster than expected: data from Kpler cited by Morgan Stanley showed 35 oil and gas tankers exited the strait on Thursday, the first time the level returned to within the typical pre-war range.

Under the Islamabad Memorandum's 60-day negotiation window, Iran agreed to let ships transit without charge. But the market dynamic has flipped from scarcity to potential oversupply. Morgan Stanley cut oil forecasts for the second time in two weeks, warning of a glut risk as China — the world's largest oil importer — has slashed imports and diversified toward Russian, Kazakh, Brazilian, and Indonesian crude. Meanwhile, more than 20 million barrels of Iranian crude are ready to sail following the temporary US sanctions waiver, with 90 percent of cargoes already on the water having no clear destination, per Bloomberg and Vortexa data. Saudi exports have rebounded to roughly 90 percent of pre-war levels.

Analysts caution the glut forecast depends on the fragile truce holding. Mohammad Reza Farzanegan of Philipps-Universitat Marburg told Al Jazeera that 'both assumptions remain fragile' — Hormuz flows and Iranian exports could be disrupted again if security deteriorates. The US struck Iran on June 27, citing a drone attack on a commercial vessel, underscoring how quickly the arrangement can fray.
sections.whyItMatters: The oil market has gone from crisis pricing to glut forecasting in weeks — but the swing is built on a 60-day ceasefire that has already been violated once. If the Doha process stalls after Khamenei's funeral, the glut narrative reverses quickly.

sections.heading: China's Rare Earth Squeeze on Japan Enters Month Six
sections.body:
China has reaffirmed its export restrictions on rare earths and dual-use technologies to Japan, now in their sixth month. On June 9, Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesman Lin Jian stated that the ban remains in place, classifying rare earths as 'dual-use materials' under Chinese law. The restrictions, originally imposed on January 6, target heavy rare earths, permanent magnets, gallium, germanium, and graphite — critical inputs for Japan's defense and semiconductor industries. Nikkei Asia reported that China's rare earth exports to Japan have plunged more than 80 percent year-on-year.

CSIS analysis notes the restrictions were triggered by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's November 2025 statement that a Chinese attack on Taiwan would constitute an 'existential threat' to Japan, potentially justifying a military response. Beijing's response ties export controls directly to the Taiwan issue — a shift from previous trade-dispute-motivated controls to ones explicitly linked to territorial security postures. The escalation followed a series of incidents in late 2025, including Chinese J-15 fighter jets locking radar onto Japanese aircraft near Okinawa and the PLA's 'Justice Mission 2025' exercises simulating a Taiwan blockade, during which 130 PLA aircraft and 14 navy ships were detected off Taiwan's coast.

The US reportedly asked Beijing to lift the restrictions, but China refused. Japan has condemned the measures as 'absolutely unacceptable,' while Defense News reported that the curbs could cripple Japan's defense manufacturing pipeline. Alternative supply chains are being explored but remain years from maturity.
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing is demonstrating that rare earth export controls are not a trade lever but a strategic weapon calibrated to a specific security objective — deterring Japan from committing to Taiwan's defense. Six months in, Japan has not reversed its stance, but the economic pressure on its defense industry is compounding.

sections.heading: NATO Gathers in Ankara Against a Reshaped Security Landscape
sections.body:
NATO leaders will convene in Ankara on July 7-8 for the alliance's first summit since The Hague in June 2025, where members committed to spending 5 percent of GDP on defense. The Ankara summit takes place against a fundamentally altered backdrop: the US-Iran war has demonstrated Europe's vulnerability to Middle Eastern energy disruptions, Russia's invasion of Ukraine has entered its fifth year with Kyiv arguably gaining momentum, and the Trump administration's relocation of troops and capabilities from Europe has strained the transatlantic partnership.

The European Policy Centre notes that the summit's agenda includes defense and deterrence planning, spending targets and burden-sharing, further assistance to Ukraine, and the future direction of the alliance. Brookings will host a pre-summit discussion on July 6 featuring analysts Asli Aydintasbas, Linas Kojala, and Matthew Kroenig, highlighting core tensions: whether European allies can deliver on 5 percent spending commitments, how the alliance manages the Trump administration's Greenland ambitions and its Iran war, and what long-term security guarantees — if any — can be offered to Ukraine.

Turkey's hosting of the summit is itself significant — Ankara has played an outsized diplomatic role in the Iran war, and its position as a NATO member bordering the Middle East gives it leverage in shaping the alliance's southern flank posture. Traffic restrictions for the summit are already in effect in Ankara.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ankara summit will test whether NATO can align on spending and strategy at a moment when the US is pulling capabilities from Europe, European allies are rearming at an unprecedented pace, and the alliance faces active conflicts on two fronts — Eastern Europe and the Middle East.

bottomLine: July 3 marks a day of extremes: Russia's largest weapons barrage on Kyiv killed at least 27 even as Ukraine's own deep-strike campaign intensifies; Iran prepares an enormous six-day funeral for Khamenei while US-Iran technical talks in Doha produce a communication channel but no breakthroughs; oil prices have fallen below $71 as Hormuz reopens faster than expected, with analysts now warning of oversupply — a remarkable swing from crisis pricing just months ago; China's rare earth embargo on Japan enters its sixth month with no resolution in sight; and NATO leaders are days from convening in Ankara to confront a security landscape that has shifted dramatically since their last gathering. The common thread across all these stories is that temporary arrangements — ceasefires, memoranda, summits — are being tested by the underlying pressures they were designed to manage.
